Line reflow at the pixel measure

A good browser wraps a long paragraph onto multiple lines that each fill the window width, breaking only at word boundaries measured in real proportional glyph advances. Without true reflow the engine wraps on an eight pixel monospace character grid while the renderer flows wider TrueType advances, so a line the grid believed would fit actually overruns the viewport and the page canvas grows sideways instead of wrapping like a mainstream browser would. This paragraph is deliberately long so the difference between a canvas that grows sideways and one that wraps to the window is obvious at a fixed width of five hundred pixels.

Proportional text such as WWWWWW versus iiiiii proves the advances differ, so measuring in real pixels is the only way to break lines where a browser breaks. The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog again and again and again and again so that this second paragraph is comfortably wider than the viewport as well.
